Fact #000818: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #000819: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #000820: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #000821: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #000822: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #000823: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #000824: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #000825: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #000826: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #000827: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #000828: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #000829: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #000830: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #000831: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #000832: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #000833: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #000834: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #000835: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #000836: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #000837: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #000838: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #000839: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #000840: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #000841: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #000842: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #000843: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #000844: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #000845: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #000846: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #000847: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #000848: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #000849: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #000850: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #000851: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #000852: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #000853: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #000854: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #000855: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #000856: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #000857: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #000858: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #000859: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #000860: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #000861: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #000862: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #000863: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #000864: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #000865: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #000866: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #000867: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #000868: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #000869: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #000870: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #000871: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #000872: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #000873: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #000874: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #000875: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #000876: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #000877: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #000878: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #000879: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #000880: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #000881: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #000882: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #000883: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #000884: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #000885: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #000886: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #000887: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #000888: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #000889: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #000890: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #000891: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #000892: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #000893: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #000894: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #000895: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #000896: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #000897: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #000898: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #000899: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #000900: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #000901: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #000902: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #000903: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #000904: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #000905: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #000906: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #000907: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #000908: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #000909: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #000910: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #000911: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #000912: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #000913: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #000914: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #000915: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #000916: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #000917: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #000918: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #000919: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #000920: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #000921: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #000922: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #000923: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #000924: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #000925: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #000926: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #000927: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #000928: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #000929: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #000930: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #000931: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #000932: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #000933: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #000934: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #000935: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #000936: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #000937: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #000938: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #000939: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #000940: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #000941: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #000942: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #000943: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #000944: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #000945: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #000946: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #000947: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #000948: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #000949: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #000950: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #000951: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #000952: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #000953: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #000954: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #000955: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #000956: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #000957: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #000958: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #000959: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #000960: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #000961: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #000962: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #000963: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #000964: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #000965: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #000966: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #000967: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #000968: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #000969: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #000970: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #000971: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #000972: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #000973: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #000974: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #000975: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #000976: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #000977: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #000978: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #000979: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #000980: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #000981: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #000982: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #000983: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #000984: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #000985: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #000986: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #000987: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #000988: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #000989: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #000990: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #000991: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #000992: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #000993: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #000994: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #000995: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #000996: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #000997: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #000998: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #000999: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #001000: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #001001: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #001002: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #001003: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #001004: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #001005: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #001006: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #001007: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #001008: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #001009: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #001010: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #001011: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #001012: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #001013: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #001014: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #001015: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #001016: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #001017: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #001018: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #001019: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #001020: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #001021: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #001022: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #001023: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #001024: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #001025: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #001026: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #001027: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #001028: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #001091: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #001092: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #001093: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #001094: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #001095: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #001096: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #001097: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #001098: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #001099: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #001100: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #001101: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #001102: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #001103: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #001104: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #001105: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #001106: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #001107: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #001108: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #001109: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #001110: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #001111: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #001112: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #001113: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #001114: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #001115: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #001116: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #001117: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #001118: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #001119: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #001120: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #001121: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #001122: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #001123: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #001124: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #001125: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #001126: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #001127: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #001128: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #001129: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #001130: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #001131: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #001132: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #001133: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #001134: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #001135: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #001136: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #001137: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #001138: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #001139: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #001140: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #001141: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #001142: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #001143: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #001144: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #001145: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #001146: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #001147: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #001148: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #001149: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #001150: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #001151: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #001152: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #001153: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #001154: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #001155: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #001156: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #001157: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #001158: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #001159: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #001160: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #001161: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #001162: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #001163: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #001164: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #001165: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #001166: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #001167: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #001168: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #001169: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #001170: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #001171: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #001172: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #001173: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #001174: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #001175: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #001176: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #001177: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #001178: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #001179: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #001180: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #001181: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #001182: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #001183: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #001184: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #001185: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #001186: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #001187: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #001188: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #001189: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #001190: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #001191: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #001192: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #001193: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #001194: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #001195: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #001196: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #001197: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #001198: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #001199: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #001200: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #001201: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #001202: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #001203: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #001204: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #001205: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #001206: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #001207: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #001208: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #001209: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #001210: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #001211: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #001212: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #001213: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #001214: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #001215: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #001216: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #001217: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #001218: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #001219: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #001220: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #001221: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #001222: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #001223: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #001224: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #001225: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #001226: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #001227: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #001228: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #001229: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #001230: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #001231: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #001232: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #001233: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #001234: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #001235: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #001236: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #001237: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #001238: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #001239: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #001240: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #001241: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #001242: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #001243: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #001244: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #001245: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #001246: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #001247: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #001248: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #001249: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #001250: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #001251: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #001252: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #001253: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #001254: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #001255: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #001256: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #001257: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #001258: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #001259: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #001260: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #001261: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #001262: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #001263: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #001264: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #001265: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #001266: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #001267: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #001268: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #001269: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #001270: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #001271: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #001272: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #001273: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #001274: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #001275: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #001276: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #001277: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #001278: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #001279: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #001280: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #001281: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #001282: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #001283: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #001284: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #001285: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #001286: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #001287: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #001288: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #001289: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #001290: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #001291: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #001292: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #001293: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #001294: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #001295: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #001296: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #001297: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #001298: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #001299: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #001362: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #001363: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #001364: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #001365: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #001366: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #001367: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #001368: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #001369: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #001370: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #001371: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #001372: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #001373: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #001374: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #001375: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #001376: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #001377: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #001378: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #001379: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #001380: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #001381: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #001382: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #001383: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #001384: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #001385: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #001386: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #001387: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #001388: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #001389: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #001390: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #001391: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #001392: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #001393: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #001394: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #001395: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #001396: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #001397: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #001398: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #001399: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #001400: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #001401: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #001402: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #001403: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #001404: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #001405: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #001406: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #001407: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #001408: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #001409: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #001410: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #001411: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #001412: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #001413: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #001414: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #001415: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #001416: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #001417: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #001418: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #001419: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #001420: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #001421: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #001422: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #001423: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #001424: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #001425: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #001426: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #001427: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #001428: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #001429: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #001430: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #001431: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #001432: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #001433: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #001434: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #001435: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #001436: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #001437: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #001438: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #001439: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #001440: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #001441: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #001442: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #001443: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #001444: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #001445: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #001446: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #001447: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #001448: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #001449: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #001450: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #001451: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #001452: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #001453: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #001454: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #001455: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #001456: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #001457: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #001458: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #001459: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #001460: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #001461: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #001462: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #001463: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #001464: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #001465: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #001466: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #001467: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #001468: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #001469: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #001470: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #001471: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #001472: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #001473: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #001474: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #001475: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #001476: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #001477: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #001478: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #001479: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #001480: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #001481: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #001482: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #001483: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #001484: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #001485: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #001486: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #001487: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #001488: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #001489: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #001490: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #001491: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #001492: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #001493: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #001494: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #001495: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #001496: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #001497: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #001498: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #001499: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #001500: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #001501: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #001502: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #001503: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #001504: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #001505: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #001506: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #001507: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.


================================================================================
  SECTION 3: GEOGRAPHY AND EARTH
================================================================================

Fact #001508: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #001509: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #001510: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #001511: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #001512: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #001513: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #001514: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #001515: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #001516: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #001517: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #001518: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #001519: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #001520: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #001521: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #001522: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #001523: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #001524: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #001525: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #001526: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #001527: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #001528: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #001529: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #001530: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #001531: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #001532: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #001533: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #001534: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #001535: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #001536: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #001537: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #001538: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #001539: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #001540: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #001541: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #001542: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #001543: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #001544: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #001545: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #001546: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #001547: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #001548: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #001549: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #001550: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #001551: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #001552: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #001553: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #001554: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #001555: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #001556: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #001557: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #001558: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #001559: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #001560: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #001561: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #001562: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #001563: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #001564: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #001565: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #001566: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #001567: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #001568: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #001569: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #001570: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #001571: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #001572: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #001573: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #001574: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #001575: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #001576: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #001577: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #001578: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.
