Fact #001635: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #001636: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #001637: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #001638: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #001639: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #001640: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #001641: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #001642: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #001643: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #001644: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #001645: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #001646: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #001647: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #001648: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #001649: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #001650: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #001651: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #001652: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #001653: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #001654: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #001655: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #001656: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #001657: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #001658: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #001659: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #001660: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #001661: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #001662: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #001663: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #001664: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #001665: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #001666: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #001667: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #001668: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #001669: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #001670: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #001671: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #001672: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #001673: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #001674: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #001675: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #001676: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #001677: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #001678: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #001679: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #001680: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #001681: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #001682: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #001683: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #001684: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #001685: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #001686: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #001687: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #001688: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #001689: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #001690: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #001691: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #001692: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #001693: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #001694: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #001695: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #001696: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #001697: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #001698: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #001699: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #001700: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #001701: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #001702: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #001703: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #001704: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #001705: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #001706: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #001707: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #001708: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #001709: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #001710: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #001711: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #001712: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #001713: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #001714: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #001715: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #001716: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #001717: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #001718: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #001719: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #001720: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #001721: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #001722: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #001723: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #001724: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #001725: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #001726: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #001727: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #001728: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #001729: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #001730: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #001731: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #001732: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #001733: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #001734: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #001735: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #001736: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #001737: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #001738: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #001739: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #001740: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #001741: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #001742: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #001743: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #001744: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #001745: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #001746: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #001747: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #001748: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #001749: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #001750: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #001751: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #001752: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #001753: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #001754: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #001755: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #001756: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #001757: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #001758: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #001759: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #001760: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #001761: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #001762: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #001763: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #001764: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #001765: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #001766: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #001767: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #001768: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #001769: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #001770: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #001771: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #001772: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #001773: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #001774: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #001775: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #001776: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #001777: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #001778: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #001779: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #001780: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #001781: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #001782: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #001783: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #001784: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #001785: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #001786: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #001787: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #001788: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #001789: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #001790: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #001791: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #001792: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #001793: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #001794: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #001795: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #001796: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #001797: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #001798: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #001799: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #001800: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #001801: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #001802: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #001803: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #001804: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #001805: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #001806: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #001807: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #001808: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #001809: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #001810: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #001811: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #001812: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #001813: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #001814: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #001815: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #001816: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #001817: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #001818: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #001819: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #001820: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #001821: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #001822: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #001823: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #001824: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #001825: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #001826: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #001827: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #001828: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #001829: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #001830: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #001831: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #001832: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #001833: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #001834: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #001835: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #001836: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #001837: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #001838: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #001839: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #001840: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #001841: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #001842: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #001843: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #001844: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #001845: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #001912: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #001913: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #001914: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #001915: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #001916: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #001917: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #001918: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #001919: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #001920: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #001921: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #001922: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #001923: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #001924: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #001925: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #001926: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #001927: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #001928: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #001929: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #001930: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #001931: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #001932: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #001933: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #001934: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #001935: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #001936: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #001937: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #001938: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #001939: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #001940: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #001941: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #001942: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #001943: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #001944: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #001945: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #001946: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #001947: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #001948: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #001949: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #001950: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #001951: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #001952: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #001953: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #001954: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #001955: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #001956: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #001957: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #001958: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #001959: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #001960: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #001961: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #001962: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #001963: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #001964: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #001965: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #001966: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #001967: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #001968: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #001969: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #001970: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #001971: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #001972: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #001973: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #001974: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #001975: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #001976: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #001977: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #001978: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #001979: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #001980: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #001981: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #001982: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #001983: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #001984: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #001985: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #001986: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #001987: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #001988: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #001989: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #001990: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #001991: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #001992: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #001993: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #001994: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #001995: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #001996: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #001997: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #001998: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #001999: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #002000: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #002001: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #002002: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #002003: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #002004: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #002005: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #002006: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #002007: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #002008: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #002009: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #002010: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #002011: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #002012: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #002013: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #002014: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #002015: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #002016: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #002017: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #002018: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #002019: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #002020: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #002021: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #002022: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #002023: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #002024: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #002025: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #002026: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #002027: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #002028: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #002029: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #002030: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #002031: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #002032: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #002033: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #002034: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #002035: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #002036: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #002037: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #002038: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #002039: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #002040: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #002041: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #002042: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #002043: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #002044: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #002045: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #002046: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #002047: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #002048: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #002049: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #002050: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #002051: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #002052: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #002053: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #002054: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #002055: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #002056: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #002057: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #002058: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #002059: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #002060: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #002061: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #002062: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #002063: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #002064: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #002065: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #002066: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #002067: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #002068: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #002069: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #002070: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #002071: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #002072: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #002073: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #002074: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #002075: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #002076: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #002077: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #002078: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #002079: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #002080: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #002081: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #002082: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #002083: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #002084: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #002085: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #002086: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #002087: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #002088: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #002089: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #002090: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #002091: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #002092: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #002093: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #002094: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #002095: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #002096: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #002097: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #002098: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #002099: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #002100: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #002101: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #002102: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #002103: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #002104: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #002105: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #002106: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #002107: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #002108: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #002109: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #002110: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #002111: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #002112: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #002113: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #002114: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #002115: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #002116: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #002117: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #002118: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #002119: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #002180: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #002181: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #002182: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #002183: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #002184: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #002185: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #002186: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #002187: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #002188: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #002189: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #002190: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #002191: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #002192: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #002193: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #002194: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #002195: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #002196: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #002197: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #002198: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #002199: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #002200: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #002201: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #002202: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #002203: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #002204: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #002205: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #002206: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #002207: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #002208: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #002209: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #002210: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #002211: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #002212: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #002213: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #002214: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #002215: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #002216: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #002217: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #002218: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #002219: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #002220: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #002221: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #002222: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #002223: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #002224: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #002225: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #002226: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #002227: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #002228: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #002229: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #002230: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #002231: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #002232: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #002233: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #002234: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #002235: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #002236: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #002237: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #002238: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #002239: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #002240: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #002241: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #002242: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #002243: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #002244: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #002245: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #002246: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #002247: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #002248: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #002249: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #002250: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #002251: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #002252: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #002253: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #002254: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #002255: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #002256: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #002257: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #002258: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #002259: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #002260: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #002261: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.


================================================================================
  SECTION 4: ANIMALS AND WILDLIFE
================================================================================

Fact #002262: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #002263: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #002264: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #002265: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #002266: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #002267: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #002268: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #002269: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #002270: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #002271: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #002272: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #002273: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #002274: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #002275: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #002276: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #002277: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #002278: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #002279: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #002280: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #002281: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #002282: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #002283: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #002284: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #002285: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #002286: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #002287: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #002288: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #002289: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #002290: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #002291: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #002292: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #002293: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #002294: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #002295: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #002296: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #002297: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #002298: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #002299: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #002300: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #002301: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #002302: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #002303: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #002304: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #002305: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #002306: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #002307: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #002308: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #002309: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #002310: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #002311: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #002312: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #002313: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #002314: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #002315: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #002316: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #002317: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #002318: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #002319: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #002320: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #002321: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #002322: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #002323: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #002324: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #002325: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #002326: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #002327: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #002328: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #002329: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #002330: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #002331: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #002332: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #002333: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #002334: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #002335: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #002336: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #002337: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #002338: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #002339: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #002340: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #002341: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #002342: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #002343: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #002344: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #002345: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #002346: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #002347: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #002348: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #002349: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #002350: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #002351: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #002352: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #002353: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #002354: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #002355: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #002356: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #002357: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #002358: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #002359: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #002360: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #002361: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #002362: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #002363: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #002364: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #002365: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #002366: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #002367: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #002368: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #002369: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #002370: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #002371: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #002372: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #002373: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #002374: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #002375: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #002376: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #002377: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #002378: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #002379: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #002380: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #002381: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #002382: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #002383: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #002384: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #002385: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #002386: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #002387: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #002388: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #002389: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #002390: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #002391: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #002392: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #002393: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #002394: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.
