Fact #003272: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #003273: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #003274: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #003275: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #003276: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #003277: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #003278: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #003279: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #003280: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #003281: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #003282: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #003283: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #003284: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #003285: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #003286: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #003287: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #003288: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #003289: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #003290: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #003291: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #003292: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #003293: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #003294: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #003295: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #003296: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #003297: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #003298: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #003299: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #003300: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #003301: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #003302: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #003303: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #003304: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #003305: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #003306: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #003307: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #003308: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #003309: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #003310: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #003311: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #003312: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #003313: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #003314: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #003315: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #003316: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #003317: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #003318: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #003319: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #003320: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #003321: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #003322: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #003323: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #003324: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #003325: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #003326: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #003327: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #003328: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #003329: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #003330: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #003331: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #003332: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #003333: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #003334: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #003335: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #003336: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #003337: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #003338: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #003339: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #003340: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #003341: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #003342: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #003343: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #003344: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #003345: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #003346: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #003347: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #003348: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #003349: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #003350: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #003351: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #003352: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #003353: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #003354: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #003355: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #003356: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #003357: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #003358: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #003359: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #003360: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #003361: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #003362: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #003363: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #003364: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #003365: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #003366: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #003367: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #003368: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #003369: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #003370: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #003371: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #003372: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #003373: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #003374: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #003375: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #003376: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #003377: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #003378: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #003379: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #003380: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #003381: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #003382: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #003383: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #003384: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #003385: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #003386: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #003387: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #003388: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #003389: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #003390: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #003391: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #003392: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #003393: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #003394: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #003395: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #003396: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #003397: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #003398: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #003399: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #003400: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #003401: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #003402: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #003403: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #003404: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #003405: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #003406: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #003407: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #003408: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #003409: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #003410: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #003411: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #003412: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #003413: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #003414: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #003415: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #003416: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #003417: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #003418: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #003419: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #003420: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #003421: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #003422: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #003423: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #003424: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #003425: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #003426: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #003427: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #003428: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #003429: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #003430: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #003431: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #003432: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #003433: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #003434: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #003435: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #003436: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #003437: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #003438: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #003439: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #003440: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #003441: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #003442: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #003443: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #003444: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #003445: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #003446: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #003447: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #003448: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #003449: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #003450: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #003451: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #003452: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #003453: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #003454: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #003455: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #003456: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #003457: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #003458: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #003459: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #003460: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #003461: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #003462: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #003463: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #003464: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #003465: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #003466: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #003467: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #003468: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #003469: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #003470: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #003471: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #003472: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #003473: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #003474: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #003475: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #003476: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #003477: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #003478: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #003479: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #003480: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #003481: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #003551: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #003552: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #003553: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #003554: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #003555: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #003556: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #003557: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #003558: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #003559: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #003560: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #003561: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #003562: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #003563: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #003564: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #003565: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #003566: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #003567: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #003568: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #003569: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #003570: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #003571: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #003572: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #003573: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #003574: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #003575: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #003576: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #003577: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #003578: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #003579: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #003580: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #003581: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #003582: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #003583: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #003584: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #003585: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #003586: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #003587: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #003588: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #003589: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #003590: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #003591: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #003592: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #003593: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #003594: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #003595: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #003596: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #003597: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #003598: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #003599: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #003600: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #003601: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #003602: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #003603: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #003604: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #003605: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #003606: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #003607: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #003608: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #003609: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #003610: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #003611: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #003612: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #003613: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #003614: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #003615: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #003616: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #003617: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #003618: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #003619: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #003620: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #003621: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #003622: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #003623: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #003624: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #003625: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #003626: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #003627: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #003628: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #003629: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #003630: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #003631: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #003632: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #003633: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #003634: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #003635: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #003636: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #003637: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #003638: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #003639: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #003640: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #003641: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #003642: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #003643: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #003644: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #003645: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #003646: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #003647: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #003648: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #003649: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #003650: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #003651: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #003652: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #003653: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #003654: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #003655: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #003656: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #003657: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #003658: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #003659: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #003660: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #003661: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #003662: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #003663: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #003664: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #003665: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #003666: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #003667: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #003668: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #003669: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #003670: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #003671: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #003672: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #003673: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #003674: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #003675: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #003676: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #003677: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #003678: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #003679: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #003680: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #003681: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #003682: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #003683: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #003684: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #003685: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #003686: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #003687: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #003688: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #003689: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #003690: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #003691: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #003692: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #003693: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #003694: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #003695: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #003696: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #003697: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #003698: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #003699: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #003700: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #003701: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #003702: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #003703: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #003704: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #003705: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #003706: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #003707: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #003708: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #003709: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #003710: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #003711: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #003712: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #003713: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #003714: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #003715: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #003716: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #003717: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #003718: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #003719: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #003720: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #003721: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #003722: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #003723: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #003724: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #003725: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #003726: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #003727: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #003728: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #003729: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #003730: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #003731: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #003732: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #003733: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #003734: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #003735: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #003736: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #003737: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #003738: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #003739: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #003740: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #003741: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #003742: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #003743: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #003744: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #003745: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #003746: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #003747: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #003748: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #003749: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #003750: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #003751: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #003752: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #003753: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #003754: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #003755: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #003756: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #003757: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #003758: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #003759: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #003760: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #003761: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #003762: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #003763: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #003764: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #003765: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #003766: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #003767: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #003768: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #003769: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #003770: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #003771: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #003772: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #003773: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #003774: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #003820: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #003821: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #003822: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #003823: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #003824: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #003825: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #003826: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #003827: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #003828: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #003829: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #003830: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #003831: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #003832: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #003833: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #003834: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #003835: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #003836: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #003837: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #003838: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #003839: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #003840: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #003841: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #003842: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #003843: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #003844: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #003845: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #003846: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #003847: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #003848: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #003849: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #003850: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #003851: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #003852: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #003853: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #003854: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #003855: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #003856: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #003857: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #003858: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #003859: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #003860: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #003861: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #003862: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #003863: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #003864: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #003865: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #003866: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #003867: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #003868: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #003869: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #003870: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #003871: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #003872: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #003873: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #003874: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #003875: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #003876: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #003877: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #003878: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #003879: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #003880: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #003881: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #003882: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #003883: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #003884: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #003885: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #003886: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #003887: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #003888: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #003889: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #003890: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #003891: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #003892: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #003893: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #003894: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #003895: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #003896: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #003897: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #003898: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #003899: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #003900: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #003901: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #003902: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #003903: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #003904: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #003905: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #003906: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #003907: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #003908: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #003909: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #003910: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #003911: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #003912: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #003913: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #003914: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #003915: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #003916: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #003917: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #003918: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #003919: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #003920: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #003921: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #003922: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #003923: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #003924: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #003925: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #003926: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #003927: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #003928: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #003929: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #003930: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #003931: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #003932: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #003933: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #003934: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #003935: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #003936: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #003937: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #003938: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #003939: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #003940: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #003941: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #003942: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #003943: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #003944: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #003945: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #003946: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #003947: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #003948: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #003949: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #003950: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #003951: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #003952: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #003953: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #003954: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #003955: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #003956: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #003957: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #003958: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #003959: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #003960: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #003961: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #003962: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #003963: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #003964: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #003965: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #003966: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #003967: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #003968: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #003969: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #003970: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #003971: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #003972: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #003973: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #003974: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #003975: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #003976: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #003977: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #003978: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #003979: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #003980: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #003981: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #003982: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #003983: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #003984: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #003985: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #003986: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #003987: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #003988: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #003989: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #003990: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #003991: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #003992: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #003993: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #003994: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #003995: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #003996: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #003997: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #003998: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #003999: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #004000: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #004001: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #004002: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #004003: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #004004: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #004005: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #004006: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #004007: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #004008: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #004009: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #004010: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #004011: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #004012: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #004013: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #004014: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #004015: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #004016: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #004017: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #004018: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #004019: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #004020: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #004021: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #004022: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #004023: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #004024: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #004025: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #004026: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #004027: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #004028: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #004029: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #004030: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.
