Fact #004095: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #004096: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #004097: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #004098: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #004099: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #004100: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #004101: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #004102: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #004103: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #004104: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #004105: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #004106: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #004107: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #004108: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #004109: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #004110: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #004111: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #004112: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #004113: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #004114: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #004115: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #004116: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #004117: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #004118: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #004119: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #004120: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #004121: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #004122: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #004123: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #004124: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #004125: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #004126: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #004127: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #004128: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #004129: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #004130: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #004131: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #004132: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #004133: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #004134: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #004135: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #004136: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #004137: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #004138: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #004139: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #004140: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #004141: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #004142: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #004143: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #004144: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #004145: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #004146: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #004147: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #004148: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #004149: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #004150: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #004151: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #004152: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #004153: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #004154: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #004155: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #004156: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #004157: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #004158: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #004159: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #004160: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #004161: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #004162: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #004163: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #004164: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #004165: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #004166: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #004167: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #004168: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #004169: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #004170: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #004171: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #004172: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #004173: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #004174: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #004175: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #004176: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #004177: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #004178: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #004179: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #004180: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #004181: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #004182: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #004183: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #004184: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #004185: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #004186: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #004187: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #004188: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #004189: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #004190: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #004191: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #004192: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #004193: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #004194: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #004195: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #004196: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #004197: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #004198: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #004199: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #004200: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #004201: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #004202: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #004203: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #004204: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #004205: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #004206: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #004207: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #004208: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #004209: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #004210: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #004211: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #004212: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #004213: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #004214: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #004215: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #004216: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #004217: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #004218: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #004219: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #004220: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #004221: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #004222: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #004223: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #004224: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #004225: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #004226: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #004227: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #004228: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #004229: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #004230: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #004231: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #004232: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #004233: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #004234: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #004235: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #004236: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #004237: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #004238: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #004239: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #004240: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #004241: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #004242: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #004243: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #004244: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #004245: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #004246: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #004247: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #004248: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #004249: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #004250: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #004251: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #004252: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #004253: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #004254: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #004255: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #004256: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #004257: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #004258: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #004259: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #004260: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #004261: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #004262: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #004263: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #004264: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #004265: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #004266: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #004267: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #004268: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #004269: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #004270: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #004271: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #004272: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #004273: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #004274: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #004275: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #004276: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #004277: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #004278: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #004279: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #004280: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #004281: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #004282: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #004283: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #004284: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #004285: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #004286: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #004287: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #004288: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #004289: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #004290: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #004291: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #004292: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #004293: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #004294: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #004295: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #004296: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #004297: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #004298: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #004299: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #004300: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #004301: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #004302: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #004303: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #004369: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #004370: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #004371: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #004372: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #004373: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #004374: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #004375: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #004376: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #004377: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #004378: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #004379: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #004380: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #004381: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #004382: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #004383: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #004384: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #004385: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #004386: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #004387: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #004388: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #004389: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #004390: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #004391: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #004392: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #004393: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #004394: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #004395: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #004396: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #004397: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #004398: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #004399: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #004400: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #004401: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #004402: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #004403: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #004404: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #004405: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #004406: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #004407: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #004408: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #004409: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #004410: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #004411: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #004412: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #004413: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #004414: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #004415: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #004416: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #004417: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #004418: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #004419: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #004420: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #004421: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #004422: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #004423: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #004424: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #004425: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #004426: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #004427: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #004428: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #004429: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #004430: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #004431: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #004432: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #004433: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #004434: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #004435: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #004436: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #004437: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #004438: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #004439: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #004440: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #004441: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #004442: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #004443: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #004444: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #004445: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #004446: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #004447: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #004448: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #004449: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #004450: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #004451: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #004452: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #004453: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #004454: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #004455: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #004456: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #004457: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #004458: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #004459: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #004460: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #004461: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #004462: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #004463: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #004464: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #004465: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #004466: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #004467: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #004468: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #004469: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #004470: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #004471: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #004472: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #004473: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #004474: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #004475: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #004476: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #004477: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #004478: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #004479: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #004480: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #004481: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #004482: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #004483: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #004484: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #004485: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #004486: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #004487: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #004488: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #004489: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #004490: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #004491: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #004492: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #004493: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #004494: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #004495: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #004496: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #004497: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #004498: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #004499: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #004500: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #004501: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #004502: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #004503: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #004504: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #004505: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #004506: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #004507: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #004508: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #004509: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #004510: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #004511: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #004512: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #004513: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #004514: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #004515: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #004516: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #004517: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #004518: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #004519: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #004520: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #004521: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #004522: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #004523: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #004524: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.


================================================================================
  SECTION 7: SPACE AND ASTRONOMY
================================================================================

Fact #004525: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #004526: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #004527: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #004528: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #004529: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #004530: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #004531: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #004532: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #004533: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #004534: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #004535: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #004536: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #004537: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #004538: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #004539: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #004540: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #004541: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #004542: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #004543: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #004544: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #004545: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #004546: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #004547: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #004548: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #004549: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #004550: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #004551: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #004552: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #004553: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #004554: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #004555: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #004556: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #004557: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #004558: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #004559: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #004560: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #004561: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #004562: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #004563: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #004564: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #004565: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #004566: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #004567: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #004568: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #004569: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #004570: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #004571: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #004572: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #004573: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #004574: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #004575: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #004576: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #004577: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #004578: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #004579: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #004580: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #004581: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #004582: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #004583: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #004638: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #004639: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #004640: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #004641: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #004642: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #004643: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #004644: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #004645: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #004646: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #004647: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #004648: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #004649: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #004650: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #004651: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #004652: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #004653: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #004654: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #004655: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #004656: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #004657: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #004658: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #004659: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #004660: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #004661: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #004662: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #004663: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #004664: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #004665: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #004666: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #004667: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #004668: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #004669: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #004670: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #004671: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #004672: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #004673: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #004674: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #004675: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #004676: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #004677: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #004678: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #004679: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #004680: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #004681: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #004682: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #004683: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #004684: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #004685: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #004686: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #004687: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #004688: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #004689: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #004690: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #004691: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #004692: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #004693: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #004694: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #004695: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #004696: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #004697: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #004698: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #004699: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #004700: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #004701: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #004702: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #004703: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #004704: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #004705: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #004706: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #004707: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #004708: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #004709: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #004710: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #004711: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #004712: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #004713: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #004714: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #004715: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #004716: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #004717: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #004718: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #004719: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #004720: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #004721: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #004722: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #004723: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #004724: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #004725: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #004726: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #004727: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #004728: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #004729: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #004730: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #004731: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #004732: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #004733: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #004734: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #004735: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #004736: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #004737: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #004738: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #004739: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #004740: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #004741: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #004742: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #004743: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #004744: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #004745: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #004746: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #004747: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #004748: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #004749: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #004750: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #004751: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #004752: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #004753: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #004754: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #004755: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #004756: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #004757: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #004758: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #004759: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #004760: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #004761: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #004762: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #004763: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #004764: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #004765: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #004766: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #004767: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #004768: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #004769: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #004770: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #004771: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #004772: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #004773: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #004774: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #004775: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #004776: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #004777: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #004778: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #004779: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #004780: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #004781: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #004782: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #004783: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #004784: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #004785: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #004786: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #004787: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #004788: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #004789: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #004790: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #004791: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #004792: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #004793: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #004794: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #004795: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #004796: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #004797: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #004798: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #004799: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #004800: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #004801: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #004802: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #004803: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #004804: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #004805: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #004806: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #004807: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #004808: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #004809: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #004810: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #004811: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #004812: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #004813: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #004814: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #004815: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #004816: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #004817: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #004818: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #004819: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #004820: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #004821: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #004822: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #004823: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #004824: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #004825: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #004826: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #004827: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #004828: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #004829: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #004830: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #004831: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #004832: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #004833: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #004834: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #004835: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #004836: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #004837: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #004838: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #004839: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #004840: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #004841: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #004842: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #004843: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #004844: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #004845: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #004846: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #004847: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #004848: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #004849: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.
