Fact #004906: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #004907: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #004908: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #004909: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #004910: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #004911: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #004912: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #004913: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #004914: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #004915: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #004916: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #004917: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #004918: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #004919: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #004920: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #004921: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #004922: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #004923: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #004924: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #004925: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #004926: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #004927: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #004928: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #004929: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #004930: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #004931: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #004932: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #004933: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #004934: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #004935: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #004936: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #004937: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #004938: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #004939: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #004940: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #004941: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #004942: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #004943: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #004944: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #004945: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #004946: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #004947: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #004948: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #004949: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #004950: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #004951: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #004952: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #004953: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #004954: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #004955: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #004956: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #004957: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #004958: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #004959: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #004960: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #004961: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #004962: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #004963: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #004964: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #004965: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #004966: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #004967: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #004968: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #004969: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #004970: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #004971: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #004972: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #004973: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #004974: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #004975: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #004976: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #004977: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #004978: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #004979: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #004980: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #004981: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #004982: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #004983: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #004984: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #004985: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #004986: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #004987: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #004988: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #004989: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #004990: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #004991: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #004992: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #004993: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #004994: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #004995: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #004996: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #004997: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #004998: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #004999: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #005000: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #005001: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #005002: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #005003: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #005004: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #005005: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #005006: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #005007: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #005008: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #005009: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #005010: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #005011: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #005012: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #005013: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #005014: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #005015: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #005016: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #005017: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #005018: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #005019: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #005020: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #005021: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #005022: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #005023: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #005024: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #005025: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #005026: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #005027: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #005028: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #005029: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #005030: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #005031: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #005032: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #005033: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #005034: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #005035: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #005036: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #005037: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #005038: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #005039: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #005040: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #005041: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #005042: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #005043: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #005044: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #005045: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #005046: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #005047: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #005048: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #005049: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #005050: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #005051: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #005052: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #005053: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #005054: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #005055: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #005056: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #005057: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #005058: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #005059: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #005060: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #005061: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #005062: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #005063: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #005064: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #005065: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #005066: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #005067: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #005068: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #005069: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #005070: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #005071: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #005072: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #005073: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #005074: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #005075: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #005076: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #005077: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #005078: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #005079: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #005080: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #005081: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #005082: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #005083: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #005084: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #005085: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #005086: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #005087: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #005088: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #005089: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #005090: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #005091: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #005092: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #005093: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #005094: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #005095: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #005096: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #005097: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #005098: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #005099: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #005100: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #005101: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #005102: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #005103: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #005104: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #005105: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #005106: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #005107: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #005108: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #005109: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #005110: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #005111: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #005112: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #005113: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #005176: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #005177: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #005178: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #005179: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #005180: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #005181: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #005182: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #005183: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #005184: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #005185: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #005186: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #005187: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #005188: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #005189: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #005190: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #005191: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #005192: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #005193: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #005194: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #005195: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #005196: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #005197: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #005198: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #005199: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #005200: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #005201: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #005202: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #005203: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #005204: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #005205: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #005206: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #005207: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #005208: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #005209: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #005210: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #005211: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #005212: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #005213: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #005214: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #005215: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #005216: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #005217: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #005218: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #005219: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #005220: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #005221: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #005222: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #005223: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #005224: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #005225: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #005226: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #005227: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #005228: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #005229: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #005230: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #005231: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #005232: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #005233: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #005234: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #005235: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #005236: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #005237: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #005238: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #005239: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #005240: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #005241: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #005242: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #005243: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #005244: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #005245: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #005246: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #005247: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #005248: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #005249: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #005250: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #005251: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #005252: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #005253: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #005254: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #005255: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #005256: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #005257: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #005258: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #005259: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #005260: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #005261: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #005262: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #005263: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #005264: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #005265: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #005266: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #005267: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #005268: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #005269: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #005270: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #005271: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #005272: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #005273: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #005274: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #005275: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #005276: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #005277: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #005278: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #005279: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #005280: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #005281: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.


================================================================================
  SECTION 8: FOOD AND DRINK
================================================================================

Fact #005282: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #005283: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #005284: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #005285: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #005286: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #005287: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #005288: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #005289: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #005290: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #005291: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #005292: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #005293: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #005294: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #005295: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #005296: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #005297: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #005298: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #005299: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #005300: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #005301: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #005302: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #005303: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #005304: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #005305: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #005306: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #005307: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #005308: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #005309: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #005310: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #005311: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #005312: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #005313: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #005314: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #005315: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #005316: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #005317: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #005318: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #005319: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #005320: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #005321: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #005322: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #005323: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #005324: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #005325: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #005326: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #005327: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #005328: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #005329: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #005330: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #005331: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #005332: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #005333: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #005334: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #005335: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #005336: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #005337: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #005338: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #005339: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #005340: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #005341: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #005342: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #005343: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #005344: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #005345: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #005346: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #005347: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #005348: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #005349: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #005350: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #005351: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #005352: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #005353: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #005354: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #005355: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #005356: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #005357: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #005358: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #005359: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #005360: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #005361: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #005362: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #005363: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #005364: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #005365: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #005366: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #005367: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #005368: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #005369: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #005370: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #005371: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #005372: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #005373: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #005374: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #005375: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #005376: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #005377: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #005378: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #005379: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #005380: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #005381: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #005382: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #005383: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #005384: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #005385: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #005386: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #005387: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #005449: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #005451: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #005452: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #005453: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #005454: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #005455: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #005456: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #005457: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #005458: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #005459: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #005460: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #005461: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #005462: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #005463: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #005464: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #005465: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #005466: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #005467: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #005468: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #005469: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #005470: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #005471: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #005472: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #005473: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #005474: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #005475: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #005476: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #005477: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #005478: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #005479: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #005480: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #005481: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #005482: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #005483: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #005484: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #005485: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #005486: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #005487: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #005488: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #005489: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #005490: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #005491: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #005492: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #005493: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #005494: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #005495: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #005496: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #005497: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #005498: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #005499: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #005500: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #005501: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #005502: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #005503: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #005504: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #005505: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #005506: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #005507: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #005508: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #005509: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #005510: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #005511: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #005512: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #005513: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #005514: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #005515: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #005516: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #005517: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #005518: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #005519: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #005520: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #005521: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #005522: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #005523: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #005524: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #005525: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #005526: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #005527: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #005528: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #005529: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #005530: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #005531: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #005532: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #005533: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #005534: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #005535: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #005536: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #005537: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #005538: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #005539: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #005540: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #005541: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #005542: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #005543: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #005544: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #005545: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #005546: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #005547: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #005548: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #005549: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #005550: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #005551: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #005552: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #005553: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #005554: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #005555: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #005556: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #005557: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #005558: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #005559: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #005560: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #005561: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #005562: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #005563: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #005564: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #005565: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #005566: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #005567: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #005568: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #005569: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #005570: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #005571: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #005572: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #005573: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #005574: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #005575: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #005576: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #005577: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #005578: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #005579: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #005580: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #005581: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #005582: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #005583: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #005584: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #005585: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #005586: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #005587: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #005588: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #005589: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #005590: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #005591: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #005592: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #005593: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #005594: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #005595: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #005596: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #005597: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #005598: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #005599: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #005600: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #005601: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #005602: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #005603: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #005604: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #005605: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #005606: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #005607: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #005608: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #005609: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #005610: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #005611: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #005612: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #005613: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #005614: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #005615: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #005616: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #005617: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #005618: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #005619: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #005620: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #005621: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #005622: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #005623: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #005624: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #005625: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #005626: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #005627: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #005628: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #005629: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #005630: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #005631: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #005632: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #005633: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #005634: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #005635: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #005636: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #005637: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #005638: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #005639: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #005640: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #005641: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #005642: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #005643: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #005644: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #005645: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #005646: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #005647: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #005648: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #005649: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #005650: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #005651: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #005652: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #005653: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #005654: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #005655: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #005656: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #005657: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.
