Fact #005716: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #005717: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #005718: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #005719: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #005720: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #005721: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #005722: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #005723: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #005724: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #005725: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #005726: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #005727: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #005728: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #005729: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #005730: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #005731: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #005732: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #005733: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #005734: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #005735: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #005736: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #005737: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #005738: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #005739: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #005740: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #005741: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #005742: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #005743: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #005744: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #005745: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #005746: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #005747: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #005748: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #005749: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #005750: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #005751: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #005752: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #005753: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #005754: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #005755: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #005756: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #005757: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #005758: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #005759: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #005760: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #005761: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #005762: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #005763: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #005764: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #005765: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #005766: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #005767: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #005768: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #005769: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #005770: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #005771: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #005772: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #005773: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #005774: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #005775: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #005776: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #005777: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #005778: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #005779: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #005780: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #005781: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #005782: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #005783: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #005784: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #005785: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #005786: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #005787: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #005788: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #005789: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #005790: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #005791: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #005792: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #005793: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #005794: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #005795: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #005796: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #005797: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #005798: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #005799: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #005800: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #005801: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #005802: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #005803: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #005804: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #005805: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #005806: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #005807: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #005808: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #005809: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #005810: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #005811: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #005812: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #005813: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #005814: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #005815: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #005816: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #005817: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #005818: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #005819: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #005820: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #005821: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #005822: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #005823: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #005824: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #005825: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #005826: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #005827: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #005828: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #005829: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #005830: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #005831: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #005832: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #005833: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #005834: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #005835: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #005836: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #005837: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #005838: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #005839: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #005840: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #005841: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #005842: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #005843: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #005844: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #005845: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #005846: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #005847: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #005848: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #005849: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #005850: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #005851: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #005852: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #005853: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #005854: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #005855: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #005856: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #005857: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #005858: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #005859: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #005860: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #005861: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #005862: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #005863: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #005864: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #005865: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #005866: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #005867: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #005868: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #005869: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #005870: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #005871: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #005872: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #005873: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #005874: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #005875: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #005876: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #005877: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #005878: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #005879: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #005880: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #005881: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #005882: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #005883: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #005884: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #005885: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #005886: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #005887: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #005888: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #005889: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #005890: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #005891: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #005892: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #005893: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #005894: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #005895: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #005896: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #005897: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #005898: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #005899: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #005900: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #005901: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #005902: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #005903: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #005904: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #005905: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #005906: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #005907: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #005908: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #005909: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #005910: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #005911: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #005912: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #005913: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #005914: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #005915: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #005916: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #005917: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #005918: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #005919: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #005920: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #005921: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #005922: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #005923: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #005924: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #005925: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #005985: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #005986: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #005987: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #005988: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #005989: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #005990: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #005991: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #005992: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #005993: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #005994: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #005995: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #005996: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #005997: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #005998: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #005999: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #006000: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #006001: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #006002: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #006003: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #006004: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #006005: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #006006: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #006007: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #006008: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #006009: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #006010: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #006011: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #006012: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #006013: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #006014: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #006015: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #006016: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #006017: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #006018: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #006019: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #006020: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #006021: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #006022: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #006023: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #006024: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #006025: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #006026: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #006027: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #006028: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #006029: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #006030: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #006031: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #006032: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #006033: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #006034: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #006035: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #006036: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #006037: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #006038: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #006039: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #006040: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.


================================================================================
  SECTION 9: PSYCHOLOGY AND BEHAVIOR
================================================================================

Fact #006041: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #006042: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #006043: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #006044: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #006045: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #006046: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #006047: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #006048: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #006049: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #006050: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #006051: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #006052: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #006053: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #006054: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #006055: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #006056: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #006057: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #006058: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #006059: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #006060: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #006061: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #006062: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #006063: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #006064: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #006065: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #006066: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #006067: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #006068: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #006069: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #006070: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #006071: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #006072: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #006073: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #006074: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #006075: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #006076: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #006077: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #006078: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #006079: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #006080: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #006081: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #006082: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #006083: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #006084: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #006085: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #006086: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #006087: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #006088: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #006089: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #006090: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #006091: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #006092: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #006093: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #006094: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #006095: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #006096: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #006097: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #006098: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #006099: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #006100: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #006101: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #006102: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #006103: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #006104: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #006105: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #006106: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #006107: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #006108: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #006109: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #006110: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #006111: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #006112: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #006113: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #006114: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #006115: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #006116: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #006117: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #006118: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #006119: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #006120: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #006121: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #006122: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #006123: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #006124: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #006125: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #006126: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #006127: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #006128: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #006129: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #006130: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #006131: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #006132: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #006133: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #006134: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #006135: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #006136: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #006137: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #006138: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #006139: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #006140: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #006141: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #006142: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #006143: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #006144: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #006145: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #006146: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #006147: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #006148: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #006149: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #006150: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #006151: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #006152: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #006153: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #006154: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #006155: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #006156: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #006157: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #006158: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #006159: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #006160: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #006161: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #006162: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #006163: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #006164: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #006165: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #006166: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #006167: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #006168: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #006169: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #006170: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #006171: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #006172: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #006173: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #006174: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #006175: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #006176: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #006177: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #006178: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #006179: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #006180: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #006181: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #006182: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #006183: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #006184: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #006185: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #006186: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #006187: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #006188: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #006189: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #006190: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #006191: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #006192: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #006193: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #006260: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #006261: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #006262: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #006263: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #006264: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #006265: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #006266: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #006267: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #006268: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #006269: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #006270: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #006271: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #006272: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #006273: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #006274: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #006275: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #006276: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #006277: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #006278: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #006279: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #006280: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #006281: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #006282: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #006283: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #006284: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #006285: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #006286: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #006287: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #006288: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #006289: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #006290: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #006291: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #006292: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #006293: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #006294: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #006295: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #006296: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #006297: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #006298: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #006299: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #006300: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #006301: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #006302: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #006303: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #006304: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #006305: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #006306: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #006307: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #006308: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #006309: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #006310: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #006311: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #006312: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #006313: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #006314: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #006315: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #006316: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #006317: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #006318: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #006319: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #006320: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #006321: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #006322: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #006323: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #006324: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #006325: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #006326: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #006327: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #006328: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #006329: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #006330: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #006331: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #006332: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #006333: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #006334: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #006335: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #006336: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #006337: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #006338: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #006339: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #006340: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #006341: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #006342: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #006343: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #006344: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #006345: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #006346: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #006347: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #006348: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #006349: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #006350: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #006351: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #006352: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #006353: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #006354: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #006355: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #006356: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #006357: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #006358: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #006359: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #006360: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #006361: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #006362: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #006363: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #006364: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #006365: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #006366: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #006367: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #006368: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #006369: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #006370: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #006371: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #006372: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #006373: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #006374: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #006375: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #006376: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #006377: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #006378: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #006379: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #006380: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #006381: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #006382: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #006383: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #006384: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #006385: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #006386: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #006387: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #006388: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #006389: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #006390: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #006391: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #006392: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #006393: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #006394: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #006395: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #006396: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #006397: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #006398: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #006399: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #006400: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #006401: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #006402: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #006403: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #006404: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #006405: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #006406: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #006407: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #006408: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #006409: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #006410: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #006411: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #006412: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #006413: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #006414: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #006415: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #006416: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #006417: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #006418: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #006419: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #006420: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #006421: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #006422: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #006423: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #006424: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #006425: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #006426: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #006427: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #006428: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #006429: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #006430: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #006431: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #006432: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #006433: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #006434: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #006435: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #006436: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #006437: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #006438: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #006439: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #006440: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #006441: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #006442: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #006443: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #006444: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #006445: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #006446: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #006447: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #006448: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #006449: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #006450: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #006451: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #006452: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #006453: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #006454: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #006455: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #006456: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #006457: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #006458: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #006459: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #006460: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #006461: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #006462: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #006463: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #006464: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #006465: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #006466: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #006467: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #006468: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.
