Fact #006528: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #006529: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #006530: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #006531: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #006532: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #006533: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #006534: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #006535: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #006536: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #006537: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #006538: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #006539: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #006540: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #006541: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #006542: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #006543: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #006544: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #006545: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #006546: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #006547: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #006548: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #006549: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #006550: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #006551: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #006552: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #006553: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #006554: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #006555: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #006556: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #006557: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #006558: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #006559: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #006560: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #006561: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #006562: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #006563: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #006564: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #006565: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #006566: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #006567: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #006568: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #006569: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #006570: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #006571: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #006572: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #006573: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #006574: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #006575: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #006576: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #006577: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #006578: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #006579: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #006580: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #006581: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #006582: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #006583: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #006584: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #006585: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #006586: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #006587: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #006588: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #006589: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #006590: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #006591: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #006592: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #006593: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #006594: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #006595: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #006596: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #006597: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #006598: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #006599: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #006600: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #006601: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #006602: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #006603: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #006604: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #006605: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #006606: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #006607: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #006608: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #006609: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #006610: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #006611: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #006612: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #006613: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #006614: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #006615: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #006616: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #006617: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #006618: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #006619: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #006620: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #006621: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #006622: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #006623: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #006624: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #006625: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #006626: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #006627: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #006628: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #006629: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #006630: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #006631: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #006632: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #006633: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #006634: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #006635: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #006636: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #006637: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #006638: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #006639: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #006640: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #006641: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #006642: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #006643: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #006644: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #006645: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #006646: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #006647: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #006648: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #006649: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #006650: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #006651: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #006652: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #006653: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #006654: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #006655: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #006656: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #006657: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #006658: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #006659: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #006660: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #006661: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #006662: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #006663: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #006664: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #006665: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #006666: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #006667: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #006668: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #006669: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #006670: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #006671: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #006672: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #006673: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #006674: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #006675: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #006676: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #006677: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #006678: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #006679: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #006680: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #006681: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #006682: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #006683: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #006684: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #006685: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #006686: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #006687: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #006688: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #006689: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #006690: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #006691: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #006692: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #006693: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #006694: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #006695: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #006696: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #006697: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #006698: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #006699: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #006700: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #006701: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #006702: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #006703: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #006704: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #006705: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #006706: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #006707: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #006708: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #006709: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #006710: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #006711: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #006712: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #006713: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #006714: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #006715: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #006716: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #006717: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #006718: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #006719: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #006720: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #006721: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #006722: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #006723: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #006724: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #006725: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #006726: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #006727: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #006728: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #006729: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #006730: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #006731: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #006732: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #006733: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #006734: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #006735: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #006736: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #006737: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #006751: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

================================================================================
  SECTION 10: MATHEMATICS AND LOGIC
================================================================================

Fact #006791: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #006792: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #006793: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #006794: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #006795: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #006796: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #006797: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #006798: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #006799: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #006800: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #006801: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #006802: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #006803: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #006804: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #006805: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #006806: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #006807: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #006808: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #006809: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #006810: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #006811: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #006812: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #006813: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #006814: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #006815: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #006816: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #006817: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #006818: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #006819: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #006820: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #006821: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #006822: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #006823: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #006824: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #006825: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #006826: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #006827: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #006828: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #006829: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #006830: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #006831: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #006832: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #006833: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #006834: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #006835: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #006836: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #006837: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #006838: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #006839: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #006840: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #006841: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #006842: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #006843: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #006844: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #006845: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #006846: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #006847: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #006848: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #006849: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #006850: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #006851: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #006852: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #006853: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #006854: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #006855: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #006856: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #006857: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #006858: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #006859: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #006860: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #006861: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #006862: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #006863: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #006864: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #006865: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #006866: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #006867: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #006868: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #006869: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #006870: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #006871: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #006872: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #006873: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #006874: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #006875: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #006876: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #006877: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #006878: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #006879: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #006880: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #006881: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #006882: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #006883: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #006884: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #006885: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #006886: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #006887: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #006888: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #006889: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #006890: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #006891: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #006892: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #006893: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #006894: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #006895: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #006896: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #006897: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #006898: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #006899: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #006900: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #006901: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #006902: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #006903: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #006904: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #006905: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #006906: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #006907: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #006908: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #006909: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #006910: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #006911: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #006912: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #006913: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #006914: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #006915: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #006916: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #006917: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #006918: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #006919: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #006920: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #006921: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #006922: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #006923: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #006924: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #006925: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #006926: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #006927: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #006928: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #006929: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #006930: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #006931: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #006932: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #006933: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #006934: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #006935: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #006936: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #006937: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #006938: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #006939: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #006940: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #006941: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #006942: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #006943: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #006944: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #006945: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #006946: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #006947: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #006948: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #006949: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #006950: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #006951: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #006952: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #006953: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #006954: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #006955: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #006956: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #006957: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #006958: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #006959: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #006960: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #006961: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #006962: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #006963: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #006964: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #006965: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #006966: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #006967: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #006968: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #006969: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #006970: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #006971: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #006972: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #006973: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #006974: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #006975: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #006976: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #006977: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #006978: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #006979: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #006980: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #006981: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #006982: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #006983: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #006984: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #006985: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #006986: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #006987: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #006988: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #006989: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #006990: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #006991: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #006992: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #006993: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #006994: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #006995: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #006996: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #006997: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #006998: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #006999: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #007000: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #007001: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #007002: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #007003: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #007063: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #007064: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #007065: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #007066: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #007067: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #007068: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #007069: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #007070: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #007071: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #007072: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #007073: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #007074: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #007075: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #007076: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #007077: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #007078: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #007079: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #007080: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #007081: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #007082: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #007083: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #007084: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #007085: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #007086: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #007087: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #007088: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #007089: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #007090: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #007091: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #007092: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #007093: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #007094: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #007095: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #007096: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #007097: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #007098: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #007099: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #007100: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #007101: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #007102: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #007103: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #007104: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #007105: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #007106: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #007107: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #007108: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #007109: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #007110: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #007111: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #007112: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #007113: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #007114: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #007115: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #007116: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #007117: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #007118: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #007119: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #007120: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #007121: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #007122: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #007123: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #007124: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #007125: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #007126: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #007127: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #007128: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #007129: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #007130: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #007131: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #007132: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #007133: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #007134: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #007135: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #007136: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #007137: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #007138: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #007139: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #007140: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #007141: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #007142: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #007143: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #007144: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #007145: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #007146: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #007147: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #007148: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #007149: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #007150: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #007151: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #007152: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #007153: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #007154: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #007155: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #007156: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #007157: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #007158: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #007159: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #007160: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #007161: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #007162: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #007163: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #007164: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #007165: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #007166: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #007167: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #007168: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #007169: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #007170: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #007171: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #007172: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #007173: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #007174: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #007175: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #007176: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #007177: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #007178: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #007179: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #007180: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #007181: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #007182: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #007183: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #007184: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #007185: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #007186: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #007187: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #007188: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #007189: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #007190: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #007191: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #007192: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #007193: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #007194: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #007195: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #007196: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #007197: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #007198: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #007199: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #007200: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #007201: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #007202: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #007203: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #007204: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #007205: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #007206: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #007207: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #007208: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #007209: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #007210: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #007211: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #007212: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #007213: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #007214: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #007215: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #007216: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #007217: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #007218: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #007219: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #007220: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #007221: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #007222: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #007223: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #007224: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #007225: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #007226: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #007227: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #007228: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #007229: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #007230: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #007231: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #007232: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #007233: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #007234: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #007235: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #007236: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #007237: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #007238: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #007239: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #007240: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #007241: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #007242: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #007243: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #007244: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #007245: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #007246: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #007247: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #007248: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #007249: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #007250: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #007251: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #007252: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #007253: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #007254: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #007255: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #007256: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #007257: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #007258: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #007259: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #007260: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #007261: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #007262: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #007263: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #007264: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #007265: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #007266: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #007267: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #007268: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #007269: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.
