Fact #007339: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #007340: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #007341: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #007342: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #007343: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #007344: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #007345: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #007346: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #007347: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #007348: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #007349: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #007350: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #007351: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #007352: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #007353: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #007354: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #007355: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #007356: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #007357: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #007358: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #007359: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #007360: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #007361: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #007362: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #007363: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #007364: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #007365: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #007366: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #007367: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #007368: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #007369: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #007370: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #007371: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #007372: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #007373: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #007374: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #007375: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #007376: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #007377: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #007378: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #007379: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #007380: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #007381: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #007382: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #007383: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #007384: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #007385: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #007386: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #007387: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #007388: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #007389: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #007390: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #007391: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #007392: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #007393: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #007394: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #007395: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #007396: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #007397: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #007398: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #007399: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #007400: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #007401: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #007402: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #007403: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #007404: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #007405: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #007406: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #007407: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #007408: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #007409: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #007410: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #007411: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #007412: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #007413: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #007414: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #007415: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #007416: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #007417: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #007418: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #007419: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #007420: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #007421: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #007422: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #007423: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #007424: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #007425: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #007426: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #007427: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #007428: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #007429: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #007430: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #007431: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #007432: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #007433: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #007434: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #007435: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #007436: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #007437: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #007438: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #007439: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #007440: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #007441: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #007442: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #007443: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #007444: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #007445: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #007446: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #007447: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #007448: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #007449: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #007450: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #007451: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #007452: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #007453: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #007454: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #007455: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #007456: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #007457: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #007458: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #007459: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #007460: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #007461: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #007462: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #007463: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #007464: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #007465: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #007466: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #007467: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #007468: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #007469: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #007470: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #007471: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #007472: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #007473: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #007474: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #007475: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #007476: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #007477: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #007478: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #007479: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #007480: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #007481: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #007482: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #007483: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #007484: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #007485: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #007486: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #007487: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #007488: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #007489: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #007490: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #007491: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #007492: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #007493: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #007494: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #007495: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #007496: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #007497: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #007498: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #007499: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #007500: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #007501: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #007502: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #007503: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #007504: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #007505: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #007506: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #007507: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #007508: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #007509: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #007510: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #007511: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #007512: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #007513: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #007514: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #007515: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #007516: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #007517: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #007518: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #007519: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #007520: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #007521: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #007522: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #007523: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #007524: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #007525: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #007526: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #007527: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #007528: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #007529: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #007530: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #007531: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #007532: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #007533: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #007534: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #007535: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #007536: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #007537: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #007538: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #007539: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #007540: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #007541: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #007542: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #007543: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #007544: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #007545: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #007546: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #007547: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #007548: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #007549: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #007605: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #007606: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #007607: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #007608: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #007609: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #007610: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #007611: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #007612: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #007613: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #007614: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #007615: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #007616: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #007617: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #007618: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #007619: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #007620: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #007621: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #007622: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #007623: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #007624: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #007625: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #007626: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #007627: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #007628: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #007629: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #007630: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #007631: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #007632: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #007633: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #007634: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #007635: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #007636: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #007637: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #007638: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #007639: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #007640: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #007641: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #007642: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #007643: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #007644: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #007645: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #007646: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #007647: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #007648: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #007649: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #007650: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #007651: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #007652: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #007653: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #007654: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #007655: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #007656: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #007657: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #007658: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #007659: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #007660: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #007661: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #007662: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #007663: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #007664: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #007665: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #007666: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #007667: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #007668: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #007669: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #007670: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #007671: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #007672: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #007673: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #007674: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #007675: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #007676: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #007677: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #007678: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #007679: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #007680: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #007681: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #007682: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #007683: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #007684: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #007685: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #007686: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #007687: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #007688: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #007689: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #007690: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #007691: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #007692: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #007693: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #007694: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #007695: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #007696: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #007697: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #007698: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #007699: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #007700: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #007701: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #007702: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #007703: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #007704: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #007705: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #007706: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #007707: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #007708: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #007709: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #007710: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #007711: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #007712: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #007713: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #007714: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #007715: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #007716: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #007717: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #007718: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #007719: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #007720: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #007721: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #007722: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #007723: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #007724: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #007725: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #007726: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #007727: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #007728: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #007729: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #007730: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #007731: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #007732: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #007733: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #007734: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #007735: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #007736: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #007737: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #007738: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #007739: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #007740: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #007741: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #007742: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #007743: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #007744: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #007745: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #007746: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #007747: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #007748: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #007749: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #007750: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #007751: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #007752: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #007753: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #007754: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #007755: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #007756: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #007757: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #007758: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #007759: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #007760: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #007761: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #007762: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #007763: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #007764: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #007765: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #007766: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #007767: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #007768: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #007769: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #007770: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #007771: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #007772: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #007773: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #007774: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #007775: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #007776: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #007777: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #007778: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #007779: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #007780: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #007781: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #007782: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #007783: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #007784: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #007785: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #007786: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #007787: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #007788: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #007789: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #007790: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #007791: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #007792: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #007793: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #007794: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #007795: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #007796: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #007797: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #007798: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #007799: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #007800: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #007801: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #007802: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #007803: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #007804: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #007805: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #007806: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #007807: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #007808: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #007809: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #007810: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #007811: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #007812: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #007813: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #007814: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #007815: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #007872: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #007873: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #007874: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #007875: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #007876: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #007877: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #007878: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #007879: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #007880: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #007881: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.

Fact #007882: There are infinitely many prime numbers, proven by Euclid over 2,000 years ago with an elegantly simple proof.

Fact #007883: The number pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion decimal places with no repeating pattern found.

Fact #007884: A googol is 10 to the power of 100, larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #007885: Zero was independently developed in India, Babylon, and among the Maya and was not widely used in Europe until the Middle Ages.

Fact #007886: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in spiral shells, sunflower seeds, and the branching of trees.

Fact #007887: Benford's Law predicts that in many naturally occurring datasets, the leading digit is 1 about 30% of the time.

Fact #007888: The imaginary number i, the square root of negative one, is essential in electrical engineering, quantum mechanics, and signal processing.

Fact #007889: The four color theorem proved in 1976 states any map can be colored with just four colors so no two adjacent regions share a color.

Fact #007890: Chaos theory shows that tiny differences in initial conditions can lead to vastly different outcomes, the so-called butterfly effect.

Fact #007891: Goedel's incompleteness theorems prove that in any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there are true statements that cannot be proven.

Fact #007892: A blue whale's tongue weighs as much as an elephant, around 2.7 metric tons.

Fact #007893: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.

Fact #007894: A group of jellyfish is called a smack.

Fact #007895: Flamingos can only eat with their heads upside down.

Fact #007896: The eyes of a giant squid can be as large as a basketball.

Fact #007897: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins, up to 40 minutes at a time.

Fact #007898: A starfish has no brain or blood, using filtered sea water to pump nutrients through its system.

Fact #007899: Crocodiles cannot stick out their tongues because a membrane holds it to the roof of their mouth.

Fact #007900: An electric eel is not an eel but is more closely related to catfish and carp.

Fact #007901: The fingerprints of a polar bear are unique, similar to how human fingerprints are unique.

Fact #007902: Penguins propose to their mates by offering them a pebble.

Fact #007903: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus in Oregon, covering about 2.4 miles.

Fact #007904: Glass is actually a supercooled liquid, not a solid, which is why ancient glass panes are thicker at the bottom.

Fact #007905: The smell of rain on dry earth has a name: petrichor, from the Greek words for stone and the fluid of the gods.

Fact #007906: Lightning strikes the Earth about 100 times every second.

Fact #007907: The core of the Earth is as hot as the surface of the Sun, around 5,500 degrees Celsius.

Fact #007908: Every year, the Moon moves about 3.8 centimeters further away from Earth.

Fact #007909: There are more bacterial cells in a human body than human cells.

Fact #007910: The longest recorded flight of a chicken is 13 seconds.

Fact #007911: Polar bears have black skin underneath their white fur to absorb heat from sunlight.

Fact #007912: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.

Fact #007913: Tigers have striped skin as well as striped fur, and no two tigers have the same stripe pattern.

Fact #007914: Horses communicate extensively through their ears. They can move each ear independently to show attention.

Fact #007915: A shrimp's heart is in its head.

Fact #007916: Spiders can survive being submerged underwater for extended periods by entering a coma-like state.

Fact #007917: The average person blinks about 15 to 20 times per minute, amounting to over 10 million blinks per year.

Fact #007918: Cats cannot taste sweetness because they lack the taste receptors for it.

Fact #007919: Dogs have a sense of smell 10,000 to 100,000 times more acute than humans.

Fact #007920: The world's largest desert is Antarctica, not the Sahara. A desert is defined by low precipitation, not by heat.

Fact #007921: Trees in a forest can communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks called mycorrhizae.

Fact #007922: The longest river in Europe is the Volga, flowing 3,531 kilometers through Russia.

Fact #007923: Iceland has no military and no mosquitoes, making it one of the most peaceful environments on Earth.

Fact #007924: The first country to give women the right to vote was New Zealand in 1893.

Fact #007925: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in Europe using movable type.

Fact #007926: The Great Fire of London in 1666 burned for four days and destroyed 13,200 houses but killed very few people.

Fact #007927: The population of Earth reached 1 billion for the first time around 1804 and is now over 8 billion.

Fact #007928: Rome was the first city in the world to reach a population of 1 million, around 50 BC.

Fact #007929: The Mongol invasion of the 13th century destroyed so many cities that some historians believe it reduced the world's population by 10%.

Fact #007930: The printing press invented by Gutenberg in the 1440s started a revolution in information sharing similar to the internet today.

Fact #007931: Glass blowing was invented in ancient Syria around 50 BC and remained the primary method for making glass vessels for over 1,500 years.

Fact #007932: Ancient Mesopotamians invented writing around 3,200 BC, primarily to keep track of grain and livestock inventories.

Fact #007933: The human appendix was once considered vestigial but is now thought to serve as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria.

Fact #007934: The average person has about 100,000 hairs on their head, and loses 50 to 100 hairs per day.

Fact #007935: Blood makes up about 7% of a person's total body weight.

Fact #007936: The olfactory bulb, which processes smells, is directly connected to the hippocampus and amygdala, which explains why smells trigger strong memories.

Fact #007937: The longest bone in the human body is the femur, which accounts for about 27% of a person's total height.

Fact #007938: Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second.

Fact #007939: The human brain uses about 20% of the body's total oxygen and energy despite being only about 2% of body weight.

Fact #007940: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #007941: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #007942: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #007943: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #007944: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #007945: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #007946: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #007947: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #007948: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #007949: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #007950: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #007951: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #007952: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #007953: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #007954: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #007955: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #007956: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #007957: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #007958: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #007959: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #007960: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #007961: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #007962: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #007963: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #007964: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #007965: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #007966: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #007967: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #007968: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #007969: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #007970: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #007971: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #007972: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #007973: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #007974: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #007975: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #007976: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #007977: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #007978: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #007979: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #007980: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #007981: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #007982: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #007983: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #007984: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #007985: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #007986: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #007987: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #007988: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #007989: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #007990: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #007991: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #007992: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #007993: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #007994: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #007995: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #007996: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #007997: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #007998: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #007999: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #008000: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #008001: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #008002: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #008003: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #008004: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #008005: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #008006: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #008007: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #008008: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #008009: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #008010: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #008011: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #008012: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #008013: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #008014: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #008015: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #008016: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #008017: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #008018: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #008019: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #008020: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #008021: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #008022: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #008023: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #008024: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #008025: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #008026: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #008027: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #008028: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #008029: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #008030: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #008031: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #008032: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #008033: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #008034: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #008035: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #008036: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #008037: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #008038: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #008039: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #008040: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #008041: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #008042: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #008043: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #008044: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #008045: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #008046: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #008047: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #008048: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #008049: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #008050: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #008051: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #008052: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #008053: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #008054: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #008055: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #008056: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #008057: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #008058: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #008059: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #008060: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #008061: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #008062: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #008063: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #008064: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #008065: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #008066: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #008067: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #008068: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #008069: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #008070: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #008071: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #008072: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #008073: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #008074: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #008075: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #008076: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #008077: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #008078: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #008079: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #008080: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.
