Fact #008149: Sweat itself is odorless. The smell associated with sweating comes from bacteria breaking down the sweat on the skin.

Fact #008150: The cornea is the only part of the human body with no blood supply. It receives oxygen directly from the air.

Fact #008151: Humans are bioluminescent, emitting light too weak to see. The light is 1,000 times less intense than the human eye can detect.

Fact #008152: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of others, forming a network more complex than any computer ever built.

Fact #008153: Light travels at approximately 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum, a constant so fundamental it defines the meter itself.

Fact #008154: DNA is so tightly coiled that if you stretched out the DNA from a single human cell, it would be about 2 meters long.

Fact #008155: The observable universe contains an estimated 2 trillion galaxies, each home to hundreds of billions of stars.

Fact #008156: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 10 million tons on Earth due to its incredible density.

Fact #008157: Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible.

Fact #008158: The human body contains enough carbon to make about 9,000 pencils.

Fact #008159: Water is the only natural substance that exists in all three states at temperatures naturally found on Earth's surface.

Fact #008160: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth, stretching over 2,300 kilometers along the Australian coast.

Fact #008161: A single bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast 100,000 slices of bread.

Fact #008162: The average cloud weighs about 1.1 million pounds, yet stays aloft because the water droplets are so tiny and spread out.

Fact #008163: Sharks are older than trees. Sharks have existed for over 450 million years, while trees only appeared about 350 million years ago.

Fact #008164: There are more possible iterations of a game of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.

Fact #008165: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the total mass of the entire solar system.

Fact #008166: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and their arms have a mind of their own with two-thirds of their neurons located in their eight arms.

Fact #008167: The loudest animal on Earth relative to its body size is the water boatman, a tiny aquatic insect producing sounds up to 99.2 decibels.

Fact #008168: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus. It takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

Fact #008169: The Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet and sees 16 types of color receptors compared to the human eye's 3.

Fact #008170: Crows can recognize human faces and hold grudges against people who have wronged them, passing this knowledge to their offspring.

Fact #008171: The total weight of all ants on Earth is roughly equal to the total weight of all humans on Earth.

Fact #008172: Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.

Fact #008173: The human eye can distinguish about 10 million different colors.

Fact #008174: An average person walks about 100,000 miles in their lifetime, more than four times around the Earth.

Fact #008175: The mitochondrial DNA in every human cell traces back to a single woman who lived in Africa approximately 150,000 to 200,000 years ago.

Fact #008176: Pluto is smaller than the United States. Its diameter is about 2,377 km while the US spans about 4,500 km coast to coast.

Fact #008177: The speed of sound in water is about 4.3 times faster than in air, which is why whales can communicate over vast ocean distances.

Fact #008178: Butterflies taste with their feet using chemoreceptors on their tarsi, helping them identify plants for laying eggs.

Fact #008179: The human stomach gets a new lining every three to four days to prevent it from digesting itself.

Fact #008180: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance. They get their pink color from carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat.

Fact #008181: The deepest point in the ocean, the Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench, is about 36,000 feet deep, deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

Fact #008182: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

Fact #008183: The Great Wall of China is not visible from space with the naked eye. Astronauts have confirmed this myth is false.

Fact #008184: Nintendo was founded in 1889 as a playing card company before pivoting to video games nearly a century later.

Fact #008185: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire. Teaching began at Oxford around 1096 AD; the Aztec Empire was founded in 1428 AD.

Fact #008186: The shortest war in history was between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasting between 38 and 45 minutes.

Fact #008187: Woolly mammoths were still alive when the Great Pyramid of Giza was being built, surviving on Wrangel Island until about 4,000 years ago.

Fact #008188: The Library of Alexandria declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple incidents rather than one catastrophic fire.

Fact #008189: Ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash because of its ammonia content and also used it in laundry.

Fact #008190: The Black Death killed so many people in 14th-century Europe that it changed the climate by allowing forests to regrow on abandoned farmland.

Fact #008191: Napoleon Bonaparte was actually of average height for his time, around 5 foot 7. The short Napoleon myth was partly British propaganda.

Fact #008192: The first photograph of a human being was taken in 1838 by Louis Daguerre when a man getting shoes shined stood still long enough.

Fact #008193: Vikings never wore horned helmets in battle. The image was popularized by 19th-century Romanticism.

Fact #008194: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be temporary and was nearly demolished in 1909 before being repurposed as a radio tower.

Fact #008195: Ancient Egyptians shaved off their eyebrows when a cat died as a sign of mourning.

Fact #008196: The Roman Empire at its peak in the 2nd century AD controlled about 5 million square kilometers and 70 million people.

Fact #008197: George Washington grew hemp on his plantation at Mount Vernon, primarily for rope and sailcloth production.

Fact #008198: The Colosseum in Rome could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators and featured a retractable awning to shade crowds.

Fact #008199: Genghis Khan's empire covered over 24 million square kilometers at its height, making it the largest contiguous land empire in history.

Fact #008200: The first Olympic Games in 776 BC held at Olympia in Greece included only a single event: a sprint of about 192 meters.

Fact #008201: Marie Curie is the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences, Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

Fact #008202: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined, containing about 60% of the world's freshwater lakes.

Fact #008203: Russia spans 11 time zones, making it the largest country by area on Earth at over 17 million square kilometers.

Fact #008204: The Amazon River discharges about 20% of all freshwater that flows into the world's oceans.

Fact #008205: Australia is the only continent that is also a single country, and it is wider than the Moon.

Fact #008206: The Sahara Desert is expanding southward at a rate of about 48 kilometers per year due to climate change and desertification.

Fact #008207: Iceland gets most of its electricity from geothermal and hydroelectric sources, making it one of the greenest countries in the world.

Fact #008208: The Dead Sea is so salty that it is almost impossible to sink in it, with salt concentration about 10 times that of ocean water.

Fact #008209: Mount Everest grows about 4 millimeters taller every year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian subcontinent pushes against Asia.

Fact #008210: The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa for about 6,650 kilometers before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea.

Fact #008211: Singapore is one of only three city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.

Fact #008212: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined, covering about 165 million square kilometers.

Fact #008213: Greenland is the world's largest island, covering about 2.1 million square kilometers, but 80% is covered by an ice sheet.

Fact #008214: The Amazon rainforest produces about 20% of the world's oxygen and is home to about 10% of all species on Earth.

Fact #008215: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area, covering about 371,000 square kilometers across five countries.

Fact #008216: Antarctica contains about 70% of the world's fresh water, locked in ice sheets up to 4.8 kilometers thick.

Fact #008217: Elephants are the only animals that cannot jump. Their leg structure and massive weight make it physically impossible.

Fact #008218: A snail can sleep for up to three years when environmental conditions are unfavorable.

Fact #008219: Wombat feces are cube-shaped, making them the only known animal to produce cubic scat.

Fact #008220: Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart in the current.

Fact #008221: The blue whale's heart is the size of a small car and beats only 4 to 8 times per minute.

Fact #008222: Tardigrades can survive in the vacuum of space, withstand extreme radiation, and endure temperatures from near absolute zero to 150 degrees Celsius.

Fact #008223: A group of owls is called a parliament.

Fact #008224: The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a cavitation bubble reaching 8,000 degrees Fahrenheit for a microsecond.

Fact #008225: Cuttlefish are colorblind but can camouflage themselves perfectly, likely using polarization of light to detect color.

Fact #008226: Axolotls can regenerate not just limbs but also heart tissue, parts of their brain, and even their spinal cord.

Fact #008227: A group of crows is called a murder. Crows can use tools, plan for the future, and solve multi-step puzzles.

Fact #008228: Horses and cows sleep standing up but can only achieve deep REM sleep lying down due to special ligaments that lock their legs.

Fact #008229: The bombardier beetle defends itself by firing boiling hot chemicals from its abdomen with a popping sound up to 500 times per second.

Fact #008230: Dolphins have unique whistles that function as names and call to specific individuals using those signature whistles.

Fact #008231: Koalas have fingerprints nearly identical to human fingerprints, similar enough to confuse crime scene investigators.

Fact #008232: The first computer bug was an actual bug, a moth found in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.

Fact #008233: The first commercially available hard drive, the IBM 350 RAMAC in 1956, stored 5 megabytes and was the size of two refrigerators.

Fact #008234: The Apollo Guidance Computer that took humans to the Moon had less processing power than a modern cheap calculator.

Fact #008235: ENIAC, one of the first electronic computers built in 1945, weighed 30 tons and used 18,000 vacuum tubes.

Fact #008236: ARPANET, the internet's predecessor, went live in 1969 connecting four universities with a 50 kbps link.

Fact #008237: Wi-Fi was accidentally invented during research into detecting mini black holes by a team at CSIRO in Australia.

Fact #008238: The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and read Merry Christmas, sent from a PC to a mobile phone.

Fact #008239: The first domain name ever registered was Symbolics.com on March 15, 1985 and it is still active.

Fact #008240: GPS satellites must account for both special and general relativity to remain accurate, otherwise GPS would drift by about 10 km per day.

Fact #008241: More transistors are produced each year than grains of rice, with modern chips containing tens of billions of transistors.

Fact #008242: Email predates the World Wide Web by about 20 years. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.

Fact #008243: The lithium-ion battery inventor John Goodenough won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2019 at age 97.

Fact #008244: The first video game ever made was Tennis for Two, created in 1958 on an oscilloscope by physicist William Higinbotham.

Fact #008245: Moore's Law, the observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years, has held consistent since Gordon Moore proposed it in 1965.

Fact #008246: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a 10th-century Danish king who united Danish tribes, just as the technology unites communication protocols.

Fact #008247: The human skeleton completely replaces itself every decade. Bone is living tissue constantly being broken down and rebuilt.

Fact #008248: The surface area of a human lung, if spread flat, would cover roughly half a tennis court.

Fact #008249: Your nose can detect over 1 trillion different smells, far more than the previously estimated 10,000.

Fact #008250: The human liver can regenerate itself completely from just 25% of its original mass.

Fact #008251: Humans share about 60% of their DNA with banana plants and 85% with mice.

Fact #008252: The femur is the strongest bone in the human body and can support up to 30 times the weight of a person.

Fact #008253: Babies have about 300 bones at birth; adults have 206. Many bones fuse together as a person grows.

Fact #008254: The average person produces enough saliva over a lifetime to fill two swimming pools.

Fact #008255: The human body emits a tiny amount of visible light, essentially glowing, but it is too faint to be seen by the naked eye.

Fact #008256: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve razor blades. The stomach protects itself with a thick mucus lining.

Fact #008257: The small intestine is about 6 meters long, three times the length of the large intestine.

Fact #008258: Every square centimeter of human skin has about 100 pain receptors, 15 pressure receptors, and 1 each for cold and warmth.

Fact #008259: Human teeth are just as hard as shark teeth, both scoring around 5 on the Mohs hardness scale.

Fact #008260: The acid in your stomach is replaced every four days to prevent the stomach from digesting itself.

Fact #008261: There are more stars in the observable universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.

Fact #008262: If you fell into a black hole, an outside observer would see you frozen in time at the event horizon.

Fact #008263: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon will remain for millions of years since there is no wind or erosion on the lunar surface.

Fact #008264: Saturn's rings span up to 282,000 km across but are typically only about 10 meters thick.

Fact #008265: Neutron stars can spin up to 716 times per second. These pulsars emit beams of radiation like cosmic lighthouses.

Fact #008266: The International Space Station travels at about 28,000 km per hour, orbiting Earth approximately every 90 minutes.

Fact #008267: There is a giant water cloud floating in space near a quasar containing 140 trillion times all the water in Earth's oceans.

Fact #008268: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, which represents about 99.86% of the solar system's mass.

Fact #008269: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object in space at over 23 billion kilometers from Earth.

Fact #008270: A year on Mercury lasts only 88 Earth days, but a day on Mercury lasts 176 Earth days.

Fact #008271: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth, meaning we always see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

Fact #008272: The largest known star, UY Scuti, is so enormous that if placed at the center of our solar system, its surface would extend beyond Jupiter.

Fact #008273: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's energy-mass content but has never been directly observed.

Fact #008274: The Milky Way galaxy is about 100,000 light-years in diameter and contains between 100 and 400 billion stars.

Fact #008275: Apples, pears, peaches, and plums are all members of the rose family, as are strawberries and raspberries.

Fact #008276: The popsicle was invented accidentally in 1905 by 11-year-old Frank Epperson who left a soda mixture with a stick outside overnight.

Fact #008277: Vanilla was originally pollinated only by a specific bee found in Mexico. Outside Mexico, vanilla orchids must be hand-pollinated.

Fact #008278: Chocolate was originally consumed as a bitter beverage by the Aztecs and Mayans. Sugar was not added until it reached Europe.

Fact #008279: Coconuts kill more people per year than sharks, with falling coconuts causing an estimated 150 deaths annually.

Fact #008280: Pineapples contain bromelain, an enzyme that digests protein, which is why your mouth tingles after eating pineapple.

Fact #008281: The first carrots were purple or yellow. Dutch growers in the 17th century developed orange carrots.

Fact #008282: Coffee was discovered, according to legend, by an Ethiopian goat herder named Kaldi who noticed his goats were energized after eating certain berries.

Fact #008283: Strawberries are not technically berries, but bananas, avocados, and watermelons are.

Fact #008284: Kopi Luwak, the world's most expensive coffee, is made from beans that have been eaten and excreted by Asian palm civets.

Fact #008285: Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, a property used by farmers to sort out unripe or rotten berries.

Fact #008286: Worcestershire sauce is made from fermented anchovies dissolved in vinegar, created in Worcester, England, in 1837.

Fact #008287: Almonds are not true nuts but are the seeds of a fruit closely related to peaches and cherries.

Fact #008288: About 4% of all cheese produced globally is stolen, making it the most stolen food in the world.

Fact #008289: An avocado is a single-seeded berry that coevolved with giant ground sloths and mammoths, which are now extinct.

Fact #008290: The Dunning-Kruger effect describes how people with low ability in a skill tend to overestimate their competence.

Fact #008291: Humans are the only animals known to blush. Darwin called blushing the most peculiar and most human of all expressions.

Fact #008292: The mere exposure effect shows that people tend to like things more simply because they have been repeatedly exposed to them.

Fact #008293: ASMR triggers a pleasurable tingling sensation in the scalp and neck in response to soft sounds.

Fact #008294: The cocktail party effect describes the brain's ability to focus on a single conversation in a noisy environment.

Fact #008295: People who are bilingual tend to switch between languages in dreams.

Fact #008296: Research shows that writing by hand improves memory and learning compared to typing because the brain encodes information more deeply.

Fact #008297: The baader-meinhof phenomenon makes you notice something everywhere after you first learn about it.

Fact #008298: Humans have a negativity bias and react more strongly to negative events than positive ones of equal magnitude.

Fact #008299: Studies show people make better decisions when they need to urinate because the self-control effort generalizes across domains.
